coredata.dc:title: Synthesis of boron-doped CQDs and its use as an additive in P3HT:PCBM layer for efficiency improvement of organic solar cell
coredata.dc:creator: Kırbıyık Kurukavak, Çisem
Yılmaz, Tuğbahan
Çetin, Şevval
Alqadasi, Mamdoh Murad
Al-Khawlany, Khawlan Mohammed
Kuş, Mahmut
coredata.dc:description: This study reports the synthesis of boron-doped CQDs (B-CQDs) and the using as a novel additive for organic solar cells. we introduced B-CQDs P3HT:PCBM bulk heterojunction organic solar cells (OSCs). The structural and morphological characterization results showed that the optical absorption and film crystallinity can be easily enhanced by B-CQDs additive in P3HT:PCBM solution. Additionally, with the improved crystallinity of P3HT, the effective interchain interaction and phase separation in photoactive layer were achieved. As a result, ~50% improving of FF of the device with 5 vol% of B-CQDs additive was determined. Thanks to improved photovoltaic characteristics, best performing device showed a power conversion efficiency of 2.7% (with ~50% improvement). This report shows that the addition of optimized vol% of B-CQDs into the photoactive blend strongly improves the absorption and leads to achieve efficient charge separation and transport in OSCs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: B-CQDs
Organic solar cells
Optical properties
Crystallinity

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the synthesis of B-CQDs.
UV–Vis absorption and photoluminesans spectra of the B‑carbon dots.
X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of P3HT:PCBM films with and w/o B-CQDs additive.
UV–Vis absorption spectra of P3HT:PCBM films with and w/o B-CQDs additive.
AFM images of P3HT:PCBM films with and w/o B-CQDs additive.
Current density-voltage curves of the best-performing OSCs under the illumination.
The calculated average crystal size and average roughness values of P3HT:PCBM films with and w/o B-CQDs additive.
Summary of best device parameters extracted from J-V curves presented in Fig. 8.
The series (Rs) and shunt resistances (Rsh) extracted from J-V curves.

head.ce:title: Synthesis of boron-doped CQDs and its use as an additive in P3HT:PCBM layer for efficiency improvement of organic solar cell

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemical Engineering, Konya Technical University, 42075 Konya, Turkey
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemical Engineering, Konya Technical University,42075, Konya, Turkey

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, Selcuk University, 42075 Konya, Turkey
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Physics, Selcuk University, 42075, Konya, Turkey

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This study reports the synthesis of boron-doped CQDs (B-CQDs) and the using as a novel additive for organic solar cells. we introduced B-CQDs P3HT:PCBM bulk heterojunction organic solar cells (OSCs). The structural and morphological characterization results showed that the optical absorption and film crystallinity can be easily enhanced by B-CQDs additive in P3HT:PCBM solution. Additionally, with the improved crystallinity of P3HT, the effective interchain interaction and phase separation in photoactive layer were achieved. As a result, ~50% improving of FF of the device with 5 vol% of B-CQDs additive was determined. Thanks to improved photovoltaic characteristics, best performing device showed a power conversion efficiency of 2.7% (with ~50% improvement). This report shows that the addition of optimized vol% of B-CQDs into the photoactive blend strongly improves the absorption and leads to achieve efficient charge separation and transport in OSCs.

ce:section.ce:para: Organic solar cells (OSCs) are a good substitute to counterparts, especially to inorganic ones. Since organic semiconductors used in OSCs are inexpensive, light weight and easily processable by solution techniques, OSCs have been progressively taking an important position in photovoltaic industry [1]. Particularly, conventional bulk heterojunction (BHJ) structure, in which photoactive layer is a bicontinuous interpenetrating network of a fullerene derivative and a semiconductor polymer, is one of the most investigated OSCs structure [2,3]. In literature, the photoactive blend layer investigated is mostly formed by using poly (3-hexylthiophene-2,5-diyl) (P3HT) and (6, 6)-phenyl C61 butyric acid methyl ester (PCBM) semiconductor materials [4,5]. There have been many reports to improve the power conversion efficiency (PCE) of conventional OSC structure including interfacial modification [6], using of different fabrication methods [7], improving of photoactive layer morphology [8], optimization of device structures [9] or incorporation of different additives [10]. Among them, incorporation of additive has been reported to be one of the most affective technique to enhance the morphology in nanometer scale. To date, different types of additive incorporated to photoactive layer have been studied including solvents [11], metallic nanoparticles [12], small molecules [13] or quantum dots (QDs) [14]. Nevertheless, there are limited reports on the addition of QDs into photoactive layer for improved OSCs, and the studies reported mostly focuses on the QDs made of lead sulphide (PbS), cadmium selenide (CdSe) or cadmium sulphide (CdS) [15]. Within QDs, carbon QDs (CQDs) have been recently applied to fabricate highly efficient OSCs.

CQDs can be easily synthesized by hydrothermal/solvothermal method, microwave heating or arc-discharge in gas phase [16–18]. Comparatively, hydrothermal method offers attractive features e.g. cost effective and eco-friendly preparation procedure. Briefly, typical hydrothermal method consists of the transferring of an organic precursor into an autoclave and the reacting at high temperature [19]. A wide range of organic precursors can be used to synthesis CQDs successfully. Depending on the precursor material and the synthesis method, CQDs show excellent properties like controllable size, optical and electrical properties. Besides, CQDs are considered to be good additives in different types of solar cell application due to their desirable photo-induced charge transfer ability [20,21]. For instance, Narayanan et al. reported that the addition of CQDs into solid-state quantum dot solar cells improved the charge transport properties, thus device performance [22]. In addition to enhanced charge transport properties, CQDs could improve the electron-hole pairs generated and suppress the charge back recombination by acting as an electron sink [23]. In recent years, doped with different hetero-atoms such as nitrogen (N), phosphorous (P) and boron (B) CQDs have been prepared and used in electronic devices as an additive [24]. In literature, it was released that the doping is particularly studied to tune the fluorescence efficacy and adjust the carbon frameworks via surface passivation of CQDs [25]. Even though, the doped CQDs studies are stinted, it was reported that doping of boron into CQDs could increase the photoluminescence (PL) quantum yield [26]. Based on heteroatom-doped carbon dots reports, it could be concluded that the introduction of B causes CQDs to have better crystal structure, which supports the conductivity enhancement, like N doped CQDs [27,28]. Taking into account all of these, the utilizing of boron doped CQDs (B-CQDs) can significantly increase the photovoltaic performance of OSCs.

With this study, we present an easy method to improve the photovoltaic parameters of conventional P3HT:PCBM OSCs by using of B-CQDs as a new additive as far as we know. B-CQDs was introduced to photoactive layer solution to enhance both of the UV-light harvesting ability and the charge transport properties. Additionally, the presence of B-CQDs could improve the formation of P3HT:PCBM crystalline structure. Due to the enhanced morphology, interchain interaction and phase separation of photoactive layer, effective charge transfer could be provided. Compared with the control device without B-CQDs additive, better crystallinity P3HT:PCBM layers with B-CQDs additive were determined, which mainly helps to increase the PCE. Thanks to ~50% improving of FF of the device with 5 vol% of B-CQDs, we achieved the PCE of 2.7% with a current density (Jsc) of 7.84 mA cm−2, an open circuit voltage (Voc) of 0.57 V and a fill factor (FF) of 48.30%. It is worth to note that increased FF values prove the better interfacial contact obtained after B-CQDs doping.

B-CQDs was prepared via boric acid, citric acid and urea (Fig. 1 ). Firstly, 4 g of boric acid (Merck), 4 g of citric acid (Sigma-Aldrich, 99%) and 4 g of urea (Sigma-Aldrich, 99%) were dissolved in 80 mL of double deionized (DI) water [18]. Then the prepared solution was subjected to hydrothermal treatment in a teflon-lined cylinder in a stainless-steel hydrothermal autoclave at 120 °C for 12 h. After cooling to room temperature, the olive green-brown colour solution was centrifuged at 6000 rpm for 10 min and the residual water was taken. The resulting solid particles was then dried using by oven at 80 °C for 24 h. Finally, the solution of B-CQDs was prepared about 10 mg/mL in chlorobenzene.

The structure of OSCs is given in Fig. 2 . Patterned indium tin oxide (ITO) glass substrates (Lumtec Corp, LT-G001 with a sheet resistance of 9–15 Ω sq.−1) were sonically cleaned by Hellmanex (Sigma Aldrich, Hellmanex III, cleaner for glass and quartz substrates) solution diluted by deionize (DI) water at the ratio of 1:50, then cleaned by DI water, acetone, and isopropyl alcohol for 10 min each. After dried with N2, all cleaned substrates were treated with oxygen plasma (Diener Plasma System Femto PCCE-Plasma Cleaner) for 5 min to remove organic impurities and activated ITO surfaces.

After filtered through a 0.45 μm PTFE, the commercial aqueous solution of poly(3,4-ethylene dioxythiophene):poly(styrene sulfonate) solution (PEDOT:PSS, Clevios PVP Al 4083) was spin coated onto the ITO surfaces at 2000 rpm for 60 s, then heated at 120 °C for 30 min. P3HT (Sigma-Aldrich):PCBM (Lumtec) bulk solution was used to prepare the photoactive layer. The solution of P3HT: PCBM was prepared in chloroform/chlorobenzene (1:1) at the ratio of 1.0:0.6 wt% (total amount of 40 mg/mL) and was stirred at 70 °C for overnight. Afterward, P3HT:PCBM blend was filtered through a 0.22 μm PTFE filter. B-CQDs were dissolved in chlorobenzene at concentration of 10 mg/mL. Then, B-CQDs solution with three different volume percentage (1%, 3% and 5%) were added into P3HT:PCBM blend. After that, P3HT: PCBM blend solution with and without B-CQDs additive was spin coated onto the PEDOT:PSS layer at 2000 rpm for 60 s. In order to remove residual solvents, all surfaces were annealed at 120 °C for 30 min. 2 nm of LiF and 80 nm of Al contacts were evaporated thermally under vacuum at a pressure of about 2 × 10−6 mbar using by physical vapour deposition (PVD) technique through a shadow mask.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Thin film and solar cell characterization
ce:section.ce:para: To identify the optical properties of B-CQDs, ultraviolet-visible (UV–Vis) absorption spectra of the solution was recorded by (Biochrom Libra S22) between 250 nm-700 nm. The light emission and intensity of B-CQDs were measured using photoluminescent (PL) spectroscopy (Perkin Elmer LS-55). The shape and particle size of B-CQDs were analysed using a Transmission Electron Microscope (TEM, JEOL, Akishima-shi, Tokyo, Japan) with 200 kV accelerating voltage. The morphological characterization of with and without B-CQDs additive P3HT:PCBM films were carried out by Atomic Force Microscope (AFM, NT-MDT NTEGRA Solaris) in “tapping” mode. X-ray diffraction (XRD) spectrum was measured using a Bruker Advance D8 X-ray diffractometer to analysed the effect of B-CQDs on the crystallization of P3HT:PCBM films. OPVs fabricated with and without B-CQDs additive were tested on ATLAS solar simulator using as AM 1.5 light source (100 mW cm−2) and a Keithley 2400 source meter with xenon arc lamp in the glovebox at the room temperature.

B-CQDs were produced in this work by a scalable hydrothermal method. The morphological and structural analyses of B-CQDs was performed by TEM and UV–Vis absorption techniques. In Fig. 3 (a) and (b), TEM images present that these B-CQDs are well dispersed and has mostly spherical form. The average diameter is calculated to be 5.4 nm. As seen in Fig. 3 (a), the large majority of B-CQDs show a uniform distribution. It could be note that B-CQDs synthesized by hydrothermal method are quantum-sized materials. In Fig. 3 (b), B-CQDs show almost spherical shape, which are well-dispersed. For further structural and optical analyses, UV–Vis absorption spectrum of the B-CQDs dispersion was recorded and the result is given in Fig. 4 . The peaks appeared at around 280 nm and 330 nm could be attributed to π–π* transition of the aromatic carbon bonds and n–π* absorption bands, respectively [29]. In addition, Figs. 4 shows photoluminesans (PL) spectrum of B-CQDs solutions. According to PL spectra, excitation at 350 nm gives a strong emission band around at 348 nm corresponding to blue PL. Under UV light, bright PL is shown as Fig. 1. Regarding PL spectrum, B-CQDs are showed an intensive fluorescent behaviour in the blue region which is correlated with the absorption–emission spectra matching with the optical properties of quantum dots [18,30–32]. Additionally, another small peak can be seen in PL spectrum. This peak could be explained by the emission of two independent B-CQDs, even at same excitation [26].

The effect of the concentration of B-CQDs in photoactive layer on the optical characteristics was determined by UV–Vis absorption spectra, seen in Fig. 6 . P3HT:PCBM-w/o B-CQDs shows a broad absorption between 350 and 650 nm. In comparison to pristine film, B-CQDs additive exhibits a significantly enhanced UV–Vis absorption in a wide region between 300 and 650 nm. After the addition of B-CQDs, all P3HT:PCBM thin films show two strong absorption peaks at around 340 nm and 510 nm. The better coverage results higher absorption with the increased vol% of B-CQDs. However, the excess vol% of B-CQDs could causes to the covering of P3HT domains, thereby leads to the decreasing of absorption [37]. Interestingly, the highest absorption was observed by adding of 3 vol% of B-CQDs. This could be related to the non-homogeneity of some points on the prepared P3HT:PCBM film. This non-homogeneity is a result of B-CQDs aggregation and it could reduce the quality of thin film morphology. As a result, this could decrease the device efficiency. Consequently, the presence and the vol% of B-CQDs in photoactive blend have strong effect on the optical absorbance properties of devices fabricated. The morphology of photoactive layer has a strong effect on the electrical properties of final device. Therefore, the morphology of P3HT:PCBM films was investigated by AFM technique and the height images were shown in Fig. 7 . In addition, the average roughness (Ra) measured by AFM software were summarized in Table 1. As seen, the Ra of P3HT:PCBM films decreased from 2.992 nm (without additive) to 1.187 nm (P3HT:PCBM-5% N-CQDs). As the charge transport properties on the photoactive layer after charge extraction is strongly related to the surface morphology, the optimization of vol% of B-CQDs additive is important to get better device performance.

The conventional BHJ structure with the configuration of ITO/PEDOT:PSS (40 nm)/P3HT:PCBM (200 nm)/LiF (2 nm)/Al (80 nm) was adopted to fabricate OSCs. To illuminate the role of B-CQDs addition into photoactive layer, we fabricated with and without B-CQDs added OSCs. The current density–voltage (J-V) curves are given in Fig. 8 and the photovoltaic characteristics extracted from these curves are listed in Table 2 . As seen, the OSCs without B-CQDs resulted a low FF (32.61%), thereby a low PCE of 1.788%. After the addition of B-CQDs, PCE of the devices with B-CQDs were significantly enhanced, and the optimized device performance reached 2.693%. The photovoltaic characteristics of best device were obtained to be a Jsc of 7.84 mA cm−2, a Voc of 0.57 V and a FF of 48.30% by adding of 5 vol% of B-CQDs. As an exception, the lower Jsc value (7.354 mA cm−2) of device prepared with 3 vol% of B-CQDs could be observed in comparison to that of OSCs without B-CQDs. This decreasing could be attributed to the morphological disordered related to aggregation domain of B-CQDs in P3HT:PCBM active layer, which is in a good matching with findings of optical absorption data [38]. Mainly, the enhanced photovoltaic performance could be attributed to the better FF values obtained after B-CQDs addition. With an exception of 3 vol% of B-CQDs added device, FF increased with the increasing vol% of B-CQDs addition. This shows that the addition of B-CQDs improves the ohmic contact, charge generation and extraction efficiency on photoactive layer [39]. For providing further information of charge transport properties, the Rs (series resistance) and Rsh (shunt resistance) were extracted from J-V curves and the results were given in Table 3 . In general trend, the Rs values decreased while the Rsh values increased with the addition of B-CQDs. The lowest Rs (20.5 Ω cm2) and the highest Rsh (318.6 Ω cm2) were obtained from the P3HT:PCBM-5% B-CQDs device. This indicates that the charge extraction and transfer properties become more vigorous, which is in a good matching with improvement of FF values [40].

To determine the standard deviations of the photovoltaic performance of the OSCs, a series of devices were fabricated. The deviations of PCEs and resistances of OSCs are given in Table 2 and Table 3 respectively. The PCEs of device fabricated with B-CQDs show small deviations in comparison to that of device without B-CQDs. The addition of B-CQDs improved the reproducibility of photovoltaic performance. As the deviations of both Rs and Rsh reduced, it could be claimed that the electrical properties can be enhanced by using of B-CQDs in photoactive layer. Such these reducing in deviations of resistance values are coherent with the improvement of PCEs thanks to surface passivation, enhanced charge carrier mobility, thus enhanced the moving of electrons and holes to the relevant transport layers. As a result, it can be concluded that more reproducible results can be obtained by the presence of B-CQDs in OSCs.

With this study, we present the synthesis and the using of B-CQDs as an additive to improve the performance of OSCs. The optimization of vol% (w/o, 1%, 3% and 5%) of B-CQDs additive in P3HT:PCBM layer was studied. By introducing B-CQDs in the photoactive layer, charge separation and transfer were enhanced. This was achieved by improved crystallinity of P3HT and optical absorption properties. The device prepared with 5 vol% of B-CQDs addition resulted the best enhancement thanks to ~50% improving of FF, which led to a 50% increasing (2.7%) over device w/o B-CQDs (1.8%). The better crystallinity and higher light harvesting boosted charge carrier mobility and transporting after charge separation. This dual advance in morphology and light harvesting illuminates the potential use of heteroatom doped CQDs to produce low-cost, solution processed and high efficiency OSCs.

Conception and design of study: Çisem Kırbıyık Kurukavak, Tuğbahan Yılmaz, Mahmut Kuş analysis and/or interpretation of data: Çisem Kırbıyık, Kurukavak, Tuğbahan Yılmaz, Mahmut Kuş, Şevval Çetin Mamdoh Murad Alqadasi, Khawlan Mohammed Al-Khawlany.

Drafting the manuscript: Çisem Kırbıyık, Kurukavak, Tuğbahan Yılmaz, Mahmut Kuş, Şevval Çetin Mamdoh Murad Alqadasi, Khawlan Mohammed Al-Khawlany revising the manuscript critically for important intellectual content: Çisem Kırbıyık Kurukavak, Tuğbahan Yılmaz.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors would like to thank to financial support of 2209-A program (PN: 1919B011903865) through the Scientific and Technological Research Council of Turkey.
